course_subtitle: Welcome to Module 4, where you will explore the innovative features and specifications of High Power Racks (HPR).
course_description_html: Welcome to Module 4, where you will explore the innovative features and specifications of High Power Racks (HPR). In this module, you will gain insights into how HPR supports next-generation workloads, including advancements in busbars, grounding, and power delivery. You will also learn about the critical design enhancements that enable efficient cooling and safety measures, ensuring optimal performance for high-density AI and HPC environments. Prepare to deepen your understanding and enhance your expertise in this essential area of modern data center infrastructure.

blocks.body_html: By the end of this module, you’ll be able to:

items_html: Describe the concept and purpose of High Power Racks (HPR) in supporting next-generation workloads
Explain the key design enhancements to busbars, grounding, and power delivery in HPR configurations
Summarize HPR specifications, including thermal improvements and liquid cooling innovations

blocks.body_html: As the demands of AI and high-performance computing accelerate, the data center industry is entering a new era of extreme density and energy consumption. This module explores how the Open Compute Project is responding through innovations like the High Power Rack (HPR) and the roadmap toward 1 megawatt racks. You’ll learn how ORv3 is evolving to meet next-gen requirements through smarter power delivery, liquid cooling integration, and scalable infrastructure design—all while maintaining the open, interoperable principles of the OCP community. Whether you're designing today’s dense AI deployments or preparing for tomorrow’s hyperscale-like workloads, this module will equip you with the key concepts and case studies shaping the future of rack infrastructure.

lessons.title: Powering the HPR: Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy

media.alt: M4-M5_Powering the HPR_Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy_Course Summary.png
media.source_asset: M4-M5_Powering the HPR_Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy_Course Summary.png

blocks.body_html: The HPR architecture significantly upgrades power capabilities over standard ORv3 racks. A key change is the busbar capacity increase—from 18 kW to a minimum of 92 kW, achieved by designing a deeper, 190 mm busbar that supports higher thermal and electrical loads.

To enable this, a welded frame extension is bolted to the standard ORv3 frame, creating space for the deeper busbar and up to six AC cable whips, each with integrated cable management. The AC input power transitioned from 20A to 30A whips in North America, while Europe maintains the 32A IEC 309 standard with dual plug configurations.

blocks.heading_html: Powering the HPR: Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy

media.alt: M4_Powering the HPR_Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy.png
media.source_asset: M4_Powering the HPR_Busbars, Grounding and Redundancy.png

blocks.body_html: Grounding improvements include a shift in the grounding point to the busbar side, which eliminates the need for ground contacts at the IT gear input connectors. The system uses low-conductivity steel in the busbar cage to raise contact resistance and mitigate ground current issues.

HPR racks can now house three power shelves and three BBU shelves, with recent plans extending support up to eight power shelves. These 1U shelves include 5.5 kW PSUs, delivering 33 kW per shelf with enhanced peak-power handling—136% for 50ms, and 160% for 400μs—making them ideal for modern AI workloads.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: Cooling is one of the most critical challenges in high-density racks. The HPR ecosystem addresses this with integrated liquid cooling and enhanced safety features that allow facilities to support high-power workloads with confidence. Explore how these innovations work together to maintain thermal balance and electrical safety at the rack level.
Click on each tab to learn more

items.title: Blind-Mate Cooling Manifolds
items.description_html: HPR racks include front-serviceable liquid cooling manifolds that are blind-mate—meaning they connect automatically when the server is slid into place. This reduces human error, minimizes leaks, and allows for easier maintenance. These connections are compliant with OCP’s cold plate interoperability guidelines, ensuring compatibility across vendors.

items.title: Liquid & Air Hybrid Design
items.description_html: While liquid cooling handles high-wattage components, airflow is still used to manage secondary heat loads. HPR racks are designed for hybrid operation, with defined hot and cold aisles and rear exhaust management. The modular manifold design supports up to six independent cold plate loops per rack.

items.title: Front Access for Maintenance
items.description_html: All cooling components in the HPR system are accessible from the front of the rack, enabling easy inspection, service, or replacement. This eliminates the need to access the rear or disturb airflow containment zones, which is especially important in large-scale deployments.

items.title: Thermal Safety Features
items.description_html: HPR racks are engineered with thermal sensors and automated safeguards to detect and mitigate overheating. If a loop fails, systems can throttle or shut down without compromising the whole rack. Leak detection and loop isolation features reduce downtime and risk.

items.title: Compliance & Certification
items.description_html: The HPR cooling design adheres to ASHRAE and OCP community standards. All components are validated for high-density use and tested for leak integrity, pressure, and thermal reliability—supporting global deployment in AI and HPC environments.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: Test your knowledge from this module. Select the best answer for each question, then submit.

blocks.question_html: What is the primary motivation behind the development of High Power Racks (HPR)?

choices.text_html: Reduce the physical footprint of server racks
Support AI workloads by boosting power capacity per rack
Replace ORv3 entirely with a new standard

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! HPR extends the ORv3 design to support higher power densities, especially for GPU and AI accelerator loads.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. HPR extends the ORv3 design to support higher power densities, especially for GPU and AI accelerator loads.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: True or False? HPR racks require a completely new data center layout to accommodate the changes.
blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! HPR retains the ORv3 mechanical footprint, allowing incremental upgrades without redesigning the layout.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. HPR retains the ORv3 mechanical footprint, allowing incremental upgrades without redesigning the layout.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: Which of the following features are part of the HPR cooling system? Select all that apply.

choices.text_html: Hot and cold aisle airflow compatibility

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! HPR supports hybrid cooling (liquid + air), features front-access service design, and includes safety measures like leak detection.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. HPR supports hybrid cooling (liquid + air), features front-access service design, and includes safety measures like leak detection.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: The HPR rack uses a [answer] volt busbar system to efficiently deliver higher current loads.
blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! HPR continues ORv3’s use of 48V direct current distribution, with enhanced busbar capacity.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. HPR continues ORv3’s use of 48V direct current distribution, with enhanced busbar capacity.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: Match the HPR component to its function.

pairs.match_html: Supports increased current capacity for AI loads
Allow liquid loop maintenance without accessing the rear
Monitor for overheating or cooling failures
Provide redundant, high-efficiency power to the IT gear

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! You have matched the HPR components with the correct function. Each component plays a critical role in supporting the HPR’s high-performance demands.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite.Take another look at how each HPR component supports the system’s function. Understanding these relationships will help clarify the architecture and its advantages in high-density environments.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: In this module, you learned how to:

items_html: Describe the purpose of High Power Racks (HPR) and their role in supporting high-density AI and HPC workloads
Explain how HPR extends ORv3 with upgraded busbars, grounding enhancements, and increased power delivery
Summarize HPR specifications, including liquid cooling integration and key safety improvements
